Everybody having a good morning? Hey, no attention to the star I've got waiting up here for a second. I'll tell you some things about George Ross that you probably know, and I'll tell you some things about George Ross that you probably don't know. One of the things about George Ross that you probably don't know is the fact that for, I think, nearly two decades, and maybe even more than two decades, he's actually teaching his course at NYU University in New York on negotiating. Another thing about George Ross that you probably don't know, I might as well get this right out of the way, is the fact that when you look at him on television, you look at him as this rough, tough guy, and he ain't that guy. He is one of the nicest people we've ever had come speak for us here at Glaser Kennedy. He's just really a very, very nice man to hear. I know, I know, I know. And he's agreed to stay beyond his talk in order to sign your books and also take photos out there. And we've got sort of the lines that are ready for that out there. So he's been very, very gracious with that. Another thing you probably don't know about George Ross, I'm about to embarrass not him, but his wife, but he's got a lovely wife, Billy, that they've been married for just a few years now. How many? 56 years. No, no, no. Hold it, hold it. If you clap, clap for her, not for me. And in the 56 years that they've been married, she has, this is the first time ever she's ever heard him speak. So this is a first for her. So she's a little nervous in the back there, and I don't know why, because George is doing all the heavy lifting up here. But a couple of things about him that you also probably do know. He's Donald Trump's chief negotiator. He's negotiated some rather small little properties, such as Trump Tower, the GM Building, the Forty Wall Street, and the Chrysler Building. He's here today to talk to you about how to deal to win with the most critical negotiation tactics and strategies that you need to know. And this is applied whether you're looking to buy a car, looking to buy a diamond, or you're looking to buy the Trump Tower, which he can also sell you today. So give a real warm Glaser, Kenny, welcome to the apprentice co-star, Mr. George Ross. Thank you. Thank you, Bill. Thank you. Oh, sit down. Thank you. Thank you. Wow. You people are crazy. I'm telling you, you're going to listen to me for an hour at that point. You ought to have your heads examined at this. But a few things we've got to get out of the way. The first thing is we've got to do a couple of apprentice items just to get them out of the way. First thing is, yes, I am taller than I look on television. You have to understand Donald is six foot three, not counting his hair. And he sits on a pedestal and I sit on the right in the basement. Second thing was, I'm a rosa as bad as she appeared on television. The answer is no, she was much worse. So much for that, but it's really very interesting. And I find it, see, I've been practicing law for more than 50 years. And not like I was under a bushel or anything at that point. Probably did more big real estate deals and big deals in New York City than any other lawyer. And nobody cares. Yeah, you're known in the industry, nobody cares. You teach a course at NYU for more than 20 years in negotiation. Who cares? You get on television, you fire some nice young kids. And he says, oh, there's George. It's got to be something wrong somewhere, but that's what it is. But let me give you a little background that you may or may not know, because we're going to talk about two different things. I'm not just going to work on negotiation today. We're also going to talk a bit on real estate, real estate investing, how to raise money, things like that. And that's what we're going to do. So I've got to give you a little background. I told you how long I've been practicing law. It's been all in real estate law. But I was five years in and I knew basically real estate law. I had an opportunity and I became involved with two multimillionaires in New York City that were really real estate at the time. This is 56 to 66. When I joined them in 1956, they had 18 properties. When I left them 10 years later, they had 720. I did 700 acquisitions of different sizes in 10 years. And they all had a lot of zeros after them. This was not small stuff. And as a result, I got exposed to a lot of good lawyers, a lot of negotiators, and I realized how bad I was. They stole everything from my jockey shorts. And I was happy doing business. They said, oh, you're great. You're doing great negotiation. But I knew that I had a lot to learn. So when I found somebody that had something good, I stole it from him. And then later on, I said, well, this negotiation is really very interesting. So why not get involved? So I started to make a study of it. And I found out, interestingly enough, the study of negotiation is only like 30 years old, 30 years old. You're negotiating every day of your life from the time you're born. When you're born at that point, you're hungry. What do you do? You cry until your mother figures out. They feed you. And then you're content. And you stop. And it's something you do every day of your life when you talk to your wife, you talk to your children, you talk to your boss. And so few people know anything about it or even try to know anything about it. Somehow they muddle through. And it's you fish that make people like me very wealthy. Because you're there to be taken advantage of, and you don't mind it. But you never made a study. And all I'm saying, I'm not going to teach you negotiation now, but I'm going to give some pointers that are going to be very helpful. And if you use them, you will find it will improve everything that you do in your life. And that's really great because it's something you can use. Now let's just talk negotiation for a minute. Are there any rules in negotiation? Can you lie? Can you cheat? Of course. Where is there something you do every day of your life? And there are no rules. Because people will not tell you what they want you, what they really believe when they're negotiating. If I looked and I showed you this watch and I said, would you like to buy this watch? And you looked and said, this watch, I love that watch. All my life I wanted that watch. I'd pay any price. Are you going to tell me that? Of course not. Because that would only raise the price. So what you'd say was, well, it's not that I got five like it, but I might buy a six one if the price were right. You lied to me. But that's what happens. That's the beauty of negotiation and also the fact of it. Negotiation is not winning. It's making a deal. Now there was a survey which was taken by one of the investigators and he contacted 150 CEOs of major corporations and said, what are the traits that you would want in someone negotiating on your behalf? List them in order of importance and tell us why. Number one trait. Number one trait of a good negotiator. Personality. Wow. Number one. And the question was why? They said, well, if you are personable, people want to do business with you. They will like you. You can make a deal. If they don't like you, they don't want to make a deal. They're unhappy. It doesn't work. So you have to train yourself to be personable. So they want to deal with you. Now, having learned this, I figured, okay, what better way to be personable than to find out something about the other side that you're negotiating with? So I used to make it a habit if I had an opportunity when I was dealing with somebody I never dealt with before that I would like to go into his or her office and see what they had on the desk around the walls because this gives you good insight. If they had a golf trophy, we'll talk golf. If they have tennis, we'll talk tennis. I went into somebody's office. This man had on his wall. I swear it was a whale. The biggest fish I ever saw. So this was easy. I said, you must love deep sea fishing. He said, I love deep sea fishing. I said, so do I. I get sick in a rowboat. But we were bonding. We were now had something in common. Until he said to me, he says, where do you go deep sea fishing? And I said, well, since I had my hip replaced, I can only do fly fishing. But we were now friends. Somehow we had a common basis for further discussions. We weren't talking business, but were we sure? We were getting to know each other. Now, second trait. This one you'll have. This one I would have thought was number one, knowledge. Knowledge of human nature. And we're going to talk about this quite a bit because this is fascinating. And that's really what you have to learn if you're going to do any marketing or presentation or sales. Human nature is wonderful. It can be relied upon. People do it every day. You can use it to your advantage. It can be used to your disadvantage if you're not aware of it. Third one. This one surprised me. Ability to organize information. Wow. So three, I said, why? And they said, well, most of you, when you have an opportunity and you're working, you're doing the same thing over and over again. If you're selling houses, you're selling houses one after another. If you're making deals, they have a tendency to be the same. How can you keep them separate if, in fact, you can't organize your information? Made sense. I did. I said, OK. I would take a class, which I did, in time management. Now, never forget the professor said, what you will do is on your desk, you will have a spiral notebook. And in that spiral notebook, where you cannot tear out the pages, you will put everything that happens in your day, who you spoke to, telephone calls, important information, goes in that spiral notebook without fail. I walked out. I said, that's a crock. Nobody does that. He's talking to somebody. I use these yellow things that stick to everything except the way you want them. And I now go. I went to Donald Trump's office and what do you think he has on his desk? A spiral notebook. What do you think I have on my desk since that day? A spiral notebook. Now, I get, there was, as you know, it's been married, very happily married for 50-some-odd years. And it was some kind of, some occasion, and a friend of my wife had seen a bracelet in Sax Fifth Avenue and explained the bracelet to me at Diamonds and Silver, what have you, and said, you wife might like it, so go look for it. First of all, Sax Fifth Avenue has got a jewelry department because of this whole rule. I finally find the bracelet. And it's a beautiful bracelet and the price is right. It's fair. I'm buying that bracelet. I can't do it. It's not my style. So I now say to the sales girl, beautiful bracelet, how much of a discount can I get? She says, sir, this is Sax Fifth Avenue. We don't give discounts. Stopped me cold. I said, maybe you don't get me somebody who does. I'd like to speak to the buyer or the manager. So she goes back, comes out, guy hugs, he says, sir, he says, you have a problem? I says, yes, you have a bracelet that I'd like to buy, but it's got a very serious floor. He says, our merchandise is perfect. What's the floor? I says, the price? Price is too high. How much of a discount can I get? He says, sir, this is Sax Fifth Avenue. We don't ordinarily give discounts. We don't ordinarily give discounts. Oh, I said, well, this is an extraordinary situation. I like the bracelet, but I don't like the price. How much of a discount can I get? So he looks, he's going to have a little problem with me. So he said, well, how much of a discount do you want? So I figured I would go easy. I said, 50%. He says, 50% I never, ever gave a 50% discount on a piece of jewelry like that. I said, no, how much of the most you ever gave? He says, 35. Are we making headway? So I said, OK, I'm at 50 or 35. Let's split the difference, make it 49. So he said, that's not splitting the difference. I said, I can't deal with somebody that's got such a fine mathematical mind. I don't even want the biggest discount you ever gave. How about I'll take 34%. So he thinks for a minute, he says, let's see, 34%. 33% is a third. And he said to me, could you possibly do it at 33%. So I thought for a minute, and I said, boy, you really drive a tough bargain. OK, for you, I'll do it. Now I said that I was going to show you how to be better negotiators in a relatively short period of time. Understand, well, first of all, I got my book out there. If you buy that, you learn a lot about negotiation. And I'm going to give you a little tip. Now, next meeting, you go into the next negotiation. Just remember this word. The post, P-O-S-T, post time, P, the people, the personalities that are going to be at this meeting or this negotiation. Who are they? What are they? What do they do? What do they know? What are their strengths and weaknesses? Now here, you have a lot of opportunity to get information. Go on the internet. Find out as much as you can about the company you're dealing with or the person that you're dealing with. You want to learn about George Roscoe? Google me. Wikipedia, look them up. You find out things that might be very helpful in relating to me. And the same thing with dealers that you're doing with, companies that you're planning on doing business, what they have, what's their product, how long have they been in business. All of this information is generally readily available to the extent that you don't have it. You're dealing with somebody you never dealt with before. Speak to somebody who's dealt with that person. And they have a good relationship buying a car, whatever it was. Get as much information as you can about the people, their strengths and their weaknesses. Now this is both on your side and the other side as much as you can. That's the P. Oh, what is the objective of this meeting? What are we trying to achieve? Now in order for it to be an objective, a true objective, it must be measurable at the end of the meeting. So if I say, well, I'm going in to buy a car. If you're not planning on buying a car, wrong objective. I'm going in to see whether the dealer has anything I might like. That's a good objective because I can see at the end of the meeting, say yes or no. If I'm going to meet a builder that's planning on building a house, do I want to see if I think I can trust them? Okay, that's a good objective. So the objective at that meeting has to be measurable so at the end of the meeting you say, I made it or I didn't make it. The S, strategy. What strategy am I going to use in this particular meeting? Am I going to appear to be stupid, smart, a lot of money, little money? What do I want to do? What strategy do I want to employ in order to achieve the result that I'm thinking of achieving? Do I want to go good guy, bad guy? Tactics, T is tactics. It's really a branch of the strategy. Now I know the strategy. What is the exact tactic that I want to use? Well, I don't want to appear anxious. So therefore, I tell my wife, when we go into the meeting, if I say I like something, you say you're not sure. If you say you think it's too expensive, I'll say, no, I think it's reasonable. Whatever it is, but we've done, we've got a tactic. We've got a game plan. Hey, okay, a game plan is the name of the game. You want to be a good negotiator, go with a game plan. Don't think, don't walk, just walk in and say, I'll shoot from the hip. Go with a game plan. Now once you start thinking about this, what do you do? You come up with the questions that the other side is going to ask you or answers that you think they're going to give you to your questions. And now you start filling in the blanks. Well, he's going to say this, and if he says this, I'm going to say that. And therefore, we'll end up now with a good negotiating. The more preparation you have in a negotiation, the more successful you will be. That's for sure. Don't be afraid to do it. Do the negotiation, do the preparation, and you'll find out that you're much more successful in all of your endeavors. Now, one thing we're going to leave you, who are the best negotiators in the world? Two-year-olds. They lie on the floor and scream until you give them what they want. Unfortunately, it's a good negotiation. You can't do that anymore. I would like to. But you can't, so therefore, you have to come up with some other plan or scheme that will work. So any questions that you want, we've got about 20 minutes. Don't be bashful. The only stupid question is the one you didn't ask. Mr. Oskar, a presentation question for you is, what book or books did you read at a young age that actually might have influenced your philosophy on business or life in general? No, that's a good question. Books, the answer is none. That's easy because I was working for these two millionaires, which were very, very good, very, very bright businessmen, and they taught me business as distinct from law. So having left them after 10 years, I now was unique. I was a lawyer that knew business, which is very rare. Lawyers usually know the law. They don't know the first thing about business. So now I could talk if a client said to me, is this a good business deal? I could make an evaluation. So I built on that, and these two billionaires, which were brilliant negotiators in their own right. They weren't that tremendously educated, but they were very good business people, and I found that. As far as negotiation sessions, the only book that I really read that I thought was pretty good was a book called Getting to Yes, written by two Harvard professors. But when I looked at it, I said, be able to really doesn't tell you what to do when somebody's impossible to deal with. How do you get to that person? So then I wrote my own book. Thank you. Hi, George. Dave Hancock. Hi, Dave. Good to see you. I invest in residential real estate now, but I have some buddies who are in commercial real estate who are telling me how much easier it is and more money. But I like what I'm doing, and I feel like I'm comfortable in that zone. But you guys are obviously investing in commercial real estate. Is that just the best sector? Is that the top tier? Is that where I should move? Or what do you recommend as you? No, good. You've got a very good question. And you bring out a lot of points, which are very important. First thing, there's no such thing as a best sector. In real estate comes in all different sizes and shapes. Each piece is entirely different. You can't say that residential is good or residential is bad because it could be good in one block and one bed and in the next block and in an area. And that's part of the problem is people have a tendency to believe what they read, and they read in generalities. So everybody says that the real estate, there's a real estate bubble. It doesn't exist. The bubble, when it breaks, you've got nothing. Real estate will run in the cycles. You catch it at the right cycle. At that point, you're going to do very well. If you catch the wrong cycle, that's okay if you can ride past it. So the idea is whatever you're planning on doing, make sure you've got enough money to do it. If you run a timeline and say, all right, I'm going to be out of here in two years. If you don't figure you've got enough money for five, and then you'll be good. And if you're doing good in residential, what particular kind, do it. Fixer uppers, fine. Four family houses, whatever it is, do it. Just do it in a manner that you're comfortable with and you can do it. Don't think that you have to be another Donald Trump or you have to go on a high rise real estate. The more you do the project, the more the risk. So pick whatever is good for you. Thank you. Hi, George. Oh, wait a minute. I've got a different voice. You've got both sides. Both sides. Hey, George. I'm Doug Ottersburg from Santa Fe, New Mexico. Got a question on syndicating deals. Is there any advantage to taking the investors and just getting all the cash together, paying cash and giving them a first mortgage on the property? Or would it be better or preferable to put that money together, use it as the down payment and give them equity and leverage it in a bank? Again, I can answer that question if you can answer one. How big is a piece of string? All right. So no answer. No, no. What I'm saying is it depends. There's a combination of both. Some investors are only interested in return. Others are interested in long-term gain, and they want to be your partners, and they don't insist upon return. And there may be a combination that you give them partially a return and partial an equity. So you have to decide what their appetite is and then basically feed it, but they're not the same. So I'm saying now, if I have somebody that only wants a return on their money, I'd love that because that's the easiest way to get rid of them. If the product goes good, here's your money, go buy a home. If I have them as an investor at that point, you can't do that. You say now you get 10% or 20% of the upside, and they're more concerned about the upside, but in which case I'm not going to give them the same amount on the return on their money on the investing side. All right. Thank you. Okay. Yeah, which side? I feel like a ping-pong ball. Hi, George. Jolie Novel, question. What's the most fun you've ever had in a negotiation? Winning. Winning, but winning, not only winning and leaving the other side feeling happy that I was a nice person to deal with and I was fair. I may have taken everything that was on the table, but they think I was fair. And then that's as long as you win and you feel comfortable about it and you can come back in and do business with them again. So you've established a relationship and they may tell their friends, well, if you ever want to do deals, use him and what have you or do it. That's the relationship. Thank you. That's the best. Which side now? George, Dave Dean Natale, it's a pleasure. I really enjoyed your talk. You mentioned that you do negotiation in the contracts for Donald Trump's licensing. Yes. Can you go into a little detail about how Donald goes about that and what kind of percentage he gets on a typical license transaction? If you asked Donald, he wouldn't know. If you ask me, I do, but it depends on the transaction. In other words, the margin in the sale of the product. Some products have a hard margin, so we'll end up with maybe a 10%, 15% royalty. Others have a low margin. You may get three or four percent. Some we've done on purely a deal. We get a share of the profits and they pick up all the expenses. So on any of these deals, Donald doesn't put a nickel in. It's some kind of guaranteed royalty over a period of years. And you just pick somebody who's good in the marketplace to do that particular product. All right. Thank you, sir. You're welcome. Yeah. Hello, Mr. Ross. Which side now? I'm over here. Okay. Janet Pellegrini Pittsburgh. It's a pleasure and honor to talk to you today. We're in an interesting situation where a big developer wants to come in and they're interested in our property that we've been running a family restaurant in for 57 years. And of course, they want it for dirt. And of course, we want dirt value. Yeah. Yeah. You know, like. Yeah, they want the land. Less than the insurance would be if it burned on. And here we are, you know, we have a business. What kind of recommendations would you make for us to be prepared with numbers? And if somebody puts a number up first, are they weaker? No. Okay. Well, let's take the first thing first. He asked me what I would do with it. I would sell the land and run and cry all the way to the bank. Because in nature of the business, you're probably getting overpaid for it, but pick a high number. I had a client who came to me that got $5 million because he was had a bar and grill in the space in New York City that would the rock center wanted to build an apartment, build an office building. So you got $5 million for lease. It was worth practically nothing. And that's what he did and worked out fine. If you like the family business and you want to keep it good, but on a long range, you can probably get a lot of money from the developer because he needs your site. And therefore you can take that money probably invested somewhere better than the what's involved in running a restaurant business. Thank you. Ken Hart, Ken from Raleigh, North Carolina. Hey, pardon? Raleigh, North Carolina. Ken Hardison. How do you respond to somebody when you say you got to do better and they say I'm not going to bid against myself? Because I run into that sometimes. Go ahead. How do you, what's your response? What was the, I didn't need to get the question. The question is when you say you got to do better, when you're in the process of negotiating and the other party says, well, I'm not going to, I'm not going to bid against myself. I'm not coming down until you come down. Yeah. I mean, do you come down or do I? Sure. Sure, sure. Take half of what you're willing to pay and use that as a number. Okay. Thanks. So at that point you say this boy, you're ready to buy it for $5,000. And the guy says, how much, and you're apart. He says it's, it's, the price is still six. He said, well, how much you want? I'll pay, give you 2,500 for it. By the time you scrape them off the ceiling, they'll probably come up with a reasonable number. Yeah. Good day, Mr. Ross. I have three quick questions to three partner. Number one, what is the mistake you've made that you could say you learned the most from? What was the lesson and what was the mistake? Also, is there anything in your business life that you could say you had a serious regret about doing or not doing? And third, do you have a, of either defining or a favorite quote? What was the last, the last one? Do you have a, do you have kind of a quote or a defining motto or kind of, you know, something that would define kind of the way you look at things? Well, okay. Let's say, the first question was that I have to do something that I didn't, that your biggest mistake, not your biggest mistake. My biggest mistake was trusting friends. That's wise. And the reason that they had a piece of, they had a piece of property that they had, it was good. They had a lease on it. And I said, okay, if I build a big apartment house on it, will you give up your lease for money? And I said, yeah, yeah, we'll be back fair. So I bought the property and when the time came fair, they gave me a number that was astronomical and they wouldn't get out and they killed the whole deal. That's wise. So that was number one. I said, don't deal with friends. I'd rather deal with the people that I'm involved with, but not friends. Second question was in your business life, was there anything you can say that you had a serious regret about doing or not doing that would influence? Doing or not doing? Yeah. Opportunity missed or? Yes. Well, let's say not doing. I can't say doing. Not doing yet. I think as serious as I didn't spend enough time doing charity work or helping people that were earlier, had less than I'd had. At a time when I could have done it, I could have afforded and put the time. When you have money, the easiest thing is to write out a check, give time to an organization. I never gave the time. I could make it, but I just didn't do it. And I wish I had done it now, making up for it. But it's a different state of affairs. I think it could have had a much greater impact on people's lives if I did it earlier. That was an unexpected but welcome answer. Thank you. The third thing was, do you have a quote or a kind of a motto, if you will, that sort of defines your view on things? Sure. What would that be? Is this failure plus perseverance equal success? Thank you very much. George, using the example of the jewelry negotiation scenario that you gave earlier, how do you define that as a win-win? And can you go back to that guy and still screw him out of the other 50 percent? Is he going to continue to want to do business? How do we incorporate the win-win? No, you are... It's not that. Understand this. Here's an expensive piece of jewelry. It's probably been sitting there. They will put it on sale the next time you're going to see it, 50 percent off or something like that to get rid of it. So they will have periodically sale numbers. So what we're saying is, well, instead of doing it waiting for the sale, why don't you do it now when you've got a fresh fish who's ready to pay or get rid of that piece of jewelry? I don't know if you're aware, but the markups which these stores have are astronomical. For example, I know that in the Trump suits, Macy's pays $136 for a suit. They sell them for $550. Come on, they can take a lot of discounts. And most of the department stores have the same... It's a similar thing. So it's no skin off his nose. Well, he could go back to... The boss say, hey, I sold a very expensive piece of jewelry. So transferring that to other deals, just your real estate deals are just any part of negotiation. Are you likely to get people to come back over and over if you go so far down? Sure. Yeah. Absolutely. Read the book. Hi, George. Hi. Wiley Long with HSA for America. I have the intention of selling my business and I have an idea in mind of what I want to sell it for. And I'm assuming that I need to start at a higher price than that. So I have some room to go down and I was wondering if you could talk about that. Absolutely right. Start as high as you like. Because somebody can say, hey, you know what I bought? I bought something that was worth a million dollars. I bought it for $200,000. Hey, what a bargain. But where did you get the million? And what's the price that you put on it? So don't be afraid. Leave yourself as much room as you can to negotiate because people will never pay the first price. Even if it's fair. If that's the price there, my bottom dollar is $500,000. They're not going to give you the bottom dollar. They're going to say you got to do better and you're back to the same thing. Any guidelines on how much to inflate it to give myself? I can't give you a guideline because it depends on a given situation. But I'll tell you an actual case that happened when I was working in the real estate broker area and somebody was buying a condominium in New York and the price was what it says was $3,008 firm. $3,008 firm. So he said, George, I'd like to buy it. What should I offer? I said, what are you planning? He said, well, I would offer, he said $3,008 firm. I would offer $3,004 firm. And he said, what would you do? I said, I would offer him $1,009 firm. And he says, they won't even listen to you to laugh at you. I said, I don't know too many people laugh about $1,009 firm. He offered $1,009 firm. He bought it for $2,400 firm. Thank you. Okay. Hi, George. This is Jeremy Shapiro with Clear River Partners. You can get a good workout up here. It's fantastic. Go ahead. My name is Jeremy Shapiro with Clear River Partners. And we help investors and successful professionals become owners with us in a 100-plus unit apartment complexes around the country. And when working with these individuals, we can set them up to become owners with us, and we'd like to talk to more folks like yourself and other professionals. Any suggestions on getting in front of more private investors and successful professionals to become partners with us? That's a good question. I don't know. There's a marketing in your particular area. I don't know. But certainly any places you can do networking is the key. So if you belong to more than one organization, then they do a lot of networking or get some kind of a good website. Word of mouth will work out more than anything else. And sometimes you catch the right connection that takes you to a number of other connections. Excellent. And any particular groups that you'd recommend that would be a good source for this, for good speaking, engagement? I make no recommendations for which I do not get paid. Hi, George. My name is Brian Urbanski. I'm the president of a local real estate investors association in Columbus, Ohio. And we're predominantly residential. And our responsibility is to provide good products and services to our members and obviously give them good advice. Historically in real estate, it's been by low, sell high. In the residential market, every market is different. In Columbus, we never had the appreciation that a lot of the coastal markets in the Sunbelt had. We still have, we're overbuilt big time. The Columbus economy isn't as good as it could be. And a lot of concerns that our members have are, you know, how much further is it going to go down? I mean, we're seeing some pretty good discounts on properties right now. But getting the funding or the financing is difficult. So now we're switching over to bank or private lenders as well as owner financing. So the question that I guess the question I have for you, after reading your book, what advice would you give residential, and I'm many to talk to you about buying several cases of those, what advice would you give residential investors in flyover country and markets that haven't seen great appreciation to buy and hold now for the long term or hold off for a little while? Because we're getting a lot of concern from our members that, you know, what if the value still drops significantly over the next year or two? A lot of people are concerned with not just Bear Stearns, but the economy as a whole. And you know, we may not have an economy in a year or two. We may go to the Euro and we may have some other things happen. So from the Emerald. What you're saying is a question of who's crystal ball you want to use. Yeah, exactly. That's all. But you never figure on buying on, when you're selling, is it really going to go higher? Or if I sell at a time, it's going to go lower? Sometimes taking the first loss is the best loss. I had a woman that didn't met me, I was speaking in San Francisco, and when she came up to me, she said, how can you say there's no real estate bubble? Six months ago, I could have sold my house for $500,000, now I can only get $450,000. So I said, well, how long have you had the house? She says 10 years. I said, you lived there? She says, yes. How much you pay for it? She says $100,000. Hey, where are you going to get something that goes from $100,000 to $450,000 in 10 years? And complain. So you don't know. At this, you do what you think is best. Same thing as stock market. Every time somebody sells a stock, somebody buys it. The person that buys it doesn't think it's going to go down. The person who sells it doesn't think it's going to go up. That's the crap shoot in real estate and most of life. Yeah. Hey, George. Pete Brady from Orange County, California. Just like Brady Bunch? The Brady Bunch. Orange County, California. You've had a lot of ups and downs. Anyhow, Trump just sold a project in Northern Baja near Rosarito. And as I understand it, it sold out. But at the same time, the Banditos basically took control of Northern Baja, all the way to the point where the mayor of Rosarito had an attempt on his life. It's a really bad situation. The state department's now not recommending that tourists even go there. I was wondering if you guys anticipated that and mitigated that risk. And I know, how do you mitigate that kind of risk? Was he anticipated? No, well, let's put it this way. We didn't send the Banditos there. Okay, so we really didn't anticipate it. But the answer is that all of those transactions, our basic input is just to give them the marketing expertise and the construction expertise and how to build and sell something of high quality. We don't have any financial investment. Somebody else has the land and has the money to do it. So if the thing goes bad, it's not really a major problem as far as we can say. We wouldn't build in some area which we don't know. That answer your question? I hope so. Okay. Yes. George, thanks for joining us today. My name is Clayton McMartin. And with 50 years experience in the real estate as an attorney, you've obviously seen a number of ups and downs and a number of cycles. Certainly we could predict what was going to happen with mortgages and these subprime loans, certainly in hindsight. But a lot of that activity was underway for many years. Where we stand today, and we're certainly, I think on a downside, can you give us some insight based on your experience of what you would expect to see next? Yeah. Good. If I had a lot of money today, I'd buy. I would buy distressed properties. I would make deals with banks. And then I would try to see if I couldn't make some kind of a deal with the owners that they could continue the property in some fashion that would work out. And they're amenable to doing that. In other words, if I were younger, which I am not, if I were younger and I had a lot of money to invest, I would invest it in New Orleans because you can buy land down there for next to nothing. And it's really a tragedy that in the ninth war, that it got wiped out and the people that lived there got wiped out. But in any other city, that would have been an area that would have been considered a blight and would have been removed. And right now, you've got plenty of land there and it will come back and it's a perfect opportunity. This is when bad times come. That's the time you can make a pot full of money if you have the courage. But I wouldn't get overextended. I mean, I wouldn't do it on a, you know, just get something you can handle. Thanks. Yes? Thank you. You're welcome. Hi, Mr. Ross. This is Bonnie Gibson from South Florida. Yeah. And I wanted to know what the best thing you have learned over the years from being with Donald Trump and what advice you would give to a new entrepreneur such as myself? Okay. That's a good question. Very good. And the only way is what I've learned from Donald Trump is to surround yourself with professionals, with people that you can trust, either accountants, lawyers, architects, anybody else that you have. And if you have the ability to delegate to professionals and you're satisfied with that, you'll do very well. Don't do it permanently. You figure I have to do everything myself. That is disaster for anybody who wants to be a major executive. Thank you. Okay. Which one? Okay. Yes. Captain Byer Rhythm, George, thank you for your wisdom. I'm the creator of the In Search of Heroes program that teaches kids to find their economic entrepreneurial hero within and make money for themselves, their families, and their community. And my question is, what is the best entity, legal entity, that helps them pay the least amount of taxes, helps them with capital accumulation, and also helps them with asset protection? Asset protection? I would say, well, you asked me about six questions in one. But I would just to say I would work with an LLC, limited liability company, where you get taxed as a partnership, but you don't have a liability of a corporation. I would do that. And I think that's good. But I would do it for, if you're dealing with real estate, each piece of real estate would be in a separate entity. So if I have a catastrophe with one, it doesn't affect the whole empire. Yeah. Okay. We're down last two, I think. Last, yeah, last two. Hello. George, my name is Ray Dyer. Okay. George, Brian from California, looking at the next one, two, three years, when do you think we're going to start seeing the real estate coming out of the trough?
